summary: Paul Graham argues that venture capitalists often behave badly not because they are inherently jerks, but because the fund structure and fee model force them to make very large investments. That pressure drives paranoia, slow decisions, idea leakage, micromanagement, and inflated valuations that can trap founders into risky outcomes.
key_points: VC behavior follows from fund economics: large funds require large deals, which magnify risk and control impulses.
Huge rounds can harm startups by forcing over-hiring and pushing valuations that limit exit options.
High valuations benefit VCs more than founders, often making IPO the only viable exit.
Top-tier VCs can behave better because they get the best deals and can fund companies that truly need big capital.
money_quotes: The real problem is the way they're paid.
This turns out to explain nearly all the characteristics of VCs that founders hate.
Like steroids, these sudden huge investments can do more harm than good.
You can't benefit from a high valuation unless you can somehow achieve what those in the business (with no apparent embarrassment) call a "liquidity event."
VCs are like car salesmen or bureaucrats: the nature of their work turns them into jerks.
key_insight: The VC model itself incentivizes large, risky, controlling behavior, so much of VC “suckage” is structural rather than personal.
